choosing adventures that excite me

6/7 this revelation unlocked massive personal freedom and energy
i don't "need" to do things just to please others
living authentically improves relationships - people respond better to my genuine self

5/7 message to this part: "thank you for protecting me. you don't have to live in the slums anymore. there's a place for you in the main house"
learning to recognize when it takes over, pause, check my feelings, communicate needs

4/7 the paradox: i'm friends with everyone, but often feel alone
universally liked, but no deep connections

3/7 but it's not alone - it's friends with my "good boy" part, the "cut-off" part, and the "shielding" part
together, they create a complex dance of over-pleasing and withdrawing, all seeking love in their own way

2/7 it pushes me out of my comfort zone, makes me say yes when i want to say no
saw this in nha trang with han - exhausted but kept going to match her excitement
this part's been running the show for years, influencing how i interact with the world

1/7 discovered a hidden part living in the slums of my mind - the people pleaser 🏚️
this teenage guardian's been shaping my life from the shadows, believing self-sacrifice is the only path to love and respect

Same goes for releasing a project asap (just to get feedback)! Done and preached countless times. 
That premature release is also derived from same fear. Build a project, have fun and when you “feel” it’s right, share it! 

Fuck playbooks, follow feelings as compass to guide you.

8. This protocol in which both these buyer apps and seller apps communicate is set by ONDC (Like HTTP protocol for internet)

9. But why do these 2 apps need to communicate? For use cases like Refund, RTO, Search,  Fulfillment, Delivery mode, etc.

(3/6)

(2/6)
4. All you do is publish your site to internet and all browsers do is follow the protocol to show your site. Your end user can find it via ANY browser!
5. Similarly, there are 2 sides in the ONDC ecosystem. Buyer Apps & Seller Apps | Communicating with defined protocols.

Explaining ONDC to 16 year old:
(1/6)
1. You publish a website to the internet.
2. Anyone with ANY browser (Chrome, Safari, Mozilla, etc) can open that website by following internet protocols (http). 
3. You don't specifically need to publish your site to every browser.

11. No True Scotsman

Jim: A Scotsman never drinks scotch with soda.
Harry: Am a Scotsman and I drink scotch with soda.
Jim: Then you must not be a true Scotsman.

12. Hasty Generalization
Far-reaching conclusions are made on the basis of an immaterial/narrow body of evidence.

10. The red herring
Distracting from the argument with a seemingly related but actually unrelated point

Eg: Worker: “Market rate is much higher than $15.”

Manager: “When I was working someplace, we got $10 per hour in much worse conditions, so you're good in there now, ask me.”

8. The False Dilemma
Presenting two choices or alternatives when there are many more that exist.

9. Burden of Proof
The lack of refuting evidence cannot be considered to be the same as supporting evidence.

7. Personal Incredulity
You are unable to understand or believe something, therefore you argue that it cannot be true.
Eg: The idea that we will ever mine for minerals on asteroids is completely ridiculous

5. The Appeal to Authority
The support of an authority figure can be a feature—but not a central pillar—of an argument. 

6. Post Hoc Ergo Propter Hoc
Just because B followed A, doesn’t necessarily mean that B was caused by A.

3. Bandwagon Fallacy
An assumption of truth on the basis of the majority of people believing it to be true.

4. Straw Man
To Ignore the actual argument and replace it with a flimsy, distorted, easily-refuted argument—a straw man

1. Ad Hominem
Attacking the individual than the argument.

2. Sunk Cost Fallacy
Continue with something on the basis of all that you've put in, with no regard given for future costs or likelihood of ultimate success.

Logical Fallacies ~ Weapons of Mass Destruction in any argument!

As a tech founder, I was subconsciously biased towards sanity. But Expansion is equally important and needed. Sales call, SEO, etc

To grow your product, you need perfect balance for 
- Expansion
- Sanity

Take example of Kingdom (AOE Inspired):
Expansion: Strong coordinated army with high tech weapons to conquer nearby towns/villages
Sanity:  Healthcare, fair markets, roads, temples, building, etc

Study
- Effects of people who are lonely is equivalent to smoking a pack of cigarettes every day. 
- People who are "chronically lonely" live 15 years shorter

Alarming Facts (US)
- The loneliest people are teenagers
- 2 out of 3 teens are chronically lonely
- 1 in 5 teens have made a suicide plan in the last year
- 1 out of 3 adults is lonely

But why
- Modern life is making us lonely. 
- Social networking is now Social media (audience-followers dopamine)
- Less physical proximity. Fewer people have families & go to churches, temples
- Office is Zoom, Shopping mall is Amazon, Grocery is Instacart, Movie Mall is Netflix, ...

We’re born unaware of our true nature, which makes us feel inadequate. This drives us to chase security and pleasure in order to fulfil this, but they’re temporary. 
In reality, we are already limitless, whole, and free. 
Key here is to remove the self-imposed boundaries that limit us and go deeper to understand self. A teacher (therapist/friends/other healers) can significantly help you to see what's already there when our lens is smoggy!

For me - it’s overwhelm feeling. 
The social part of me hijacks (cause he loves the other person(s) so much) and the ceo part of me is overwhelmed to listen and fulfil his requests. 
Other parts are angry on the ceo for not giving them time for ‘myself’ which makes ceo even more overwhelmed. 

Tell for these are I am anxious, rushed, urgency feeling. 

I have to constantly check myself on how all parts of me are feeling. Somehow I forget to check and have a ‘round table conference’ of all my parts which makes me not notice these tells.

What a powerful reminder - 

Before you walk into any space, remind yourself that you need NOTHING.   

You are whole. You are complete. You are here to be fully open and experience the people in the room without any expectation.  

You are not here to make friends. You are not here to find a date. You are not here to make a business connection. You are not here to look smart, sexy, funny, cool. You need nothing from no one.  

You are simply HERE.  Check your own scarcity at the door. Take it off like a jacket and forget about it. Shift into the mindset of someone who is here to give, to love, to listen, to play, to speak freely and trust the words that come out without judging them, to make people feel GOOD and seen and heard.  

 OPEN YOUR HEART AND MIND TO EVERY PERSON YOU SEE.

I was ALWAYS anxious back in my Blr startup days. 

At a club - oh I have to ask out any girl. It’s a numbers game!! Forget about enjoying the gig and vibe.

At a conference - oh I have to find how someone can be of help in my business. What do they know that can help me. Who can they connect me to who can tell me more! Even forgot multiple times about eating anything! 

At a house party - oh I have to make others think I am cool! Sometimes a J would help calm my anxious part and get other parts a driving seat but without it, I’m unconfident! 

I am glad of where I am today. Now, I just be! I’m there to fully experience without any expectation. Flowing with energy. It is like an art festival. These events are roller coasters for my Kid parts now and they have their fun. For my healer part, to be out and help people. 

Thank you, me!

This morning I questioned everything I've been building for months.

Walking in my garden, my mind flooded with other people's ideas from Twitter where I saw someone's voice to notion app doing really well. Clean. Productive. Clear value prop: "Save time at work."

And a voice in me was, doubting everything.

"Should I have built that instead of WhatsHappening? Look how well it is doing. Can I integrate this feature? Should I pivot?"

But as I kept walking, watching small birds hopping between branches, saying all my thoughts in WhatsHappening ; something shifted. I felt more confident in my chest. More relaxed. More grounded.

After voicing this fear, other voices started to bring me back. They reminded me that WhatsHappening isn't about saving time. It's a safe space for all the voices inside us to come out. It's about getting thoughts out of our heads so we can actually live with ourselves. It's about this ritual of dialogue in inner voices.

When I voice journal like this, I feel more ME. More content. More present.

The thought loops that would've stayed stuck in my head for hours? They're out now. I'm free to enjoy the birds, the morning, this moment.

Building in the mountains, walking, thinking, getting worries out of my head and turning them into something real - this feels like the writer's life I really want.

I built WhatsHappening to give you back "yourself"

Early career years are painful. 
 
You feel like an idiot 98% of the time - lost, confused and insecure.

Built an amazing product in 1.5 years without speaking to a single customer
--> resulted in my first failed startup.

Got 1 paying customer before building anything, built a crappy MVP over the weekend after they paid
--> 3.5 years later we have paying customers in 75 countries.

Building a startup is easier when you have a productive routine.
Here are some of the simple productivity hacks to build a better routine: A [Thread🧵].....

[7] Hack #7 – Create a highlight every day
 
Always create one highlight of the day.
 
One task that you have to do no matter what.
 
One task that will bring you joy and makes you feel accomplished at the end of the day.
 
I do this every day and the result is always satisfying.

Have a corner/space for everything.
 
If you mix it up, you’ll feel tired and not productive.
 
Your surroundings have a vibe attached to them.
 
If you work where you sleep, you subconsciously will want to sleep or take a quick nap.

[4] Hack #4 – Organize your surroundings
 
There is a time and place for everything.
 
Don’t eat where you work,
Don’t work, where you sleep,
Don’t sleep, where you play,
Don’t play where you eat.

Be your authentic self and lead from that place.

Note to Self:

You have become a master at avoiding distractions in life with two skills.

- intentional creation/removal of friction
- Ready alternatives.

We spend years building elaborate structures to shield ourselves from pain. 

Analyze enough, and we'll feel safe. 

Plan thoroughly, and we'll believe we can never be caught off guard. 

But safety and aliveness rarely coexist. 

At some point, we must choose which one to serve.
Your fear of being seen isn't protecting you - it's protecting the world from receiving your medicine. And we need your medicine now more than ever.

The "perfect niche" you're searching for isn't a demographic or pain point - it's a frequency. Your people will find you when you stop trying to appeal to everyone and start resonating at your natural depth.

You want to go to war, sure, I'm not stopping you. 

But aim to be the fucking general, not the sword in some rando's hand.

I backslide into this kind of trap every so often. Not necessarily the same one, but some equivalent, where you walk into a cage of someone else's making because of fear or loneliness or anxiety or ignorance. 

Your best bet is to have external advisors who will call it out.

Especially early 20 somethings... You've spent your whole life in an education system designed to turn you into someone else's weapon. 

Then you get out, get into tech, start making money... and create another prison with the same structure as school/college for yourself?

If you have an objective that requires you to level up, sure, fuck, go for it.

But if you're levelling up just because, you're trapped in someone else's game.

Can't you smell the stink of gamification designed to turn you into someone else's weapon?

You're a fucking human being experiencing life, not a target for a mindless optimization process.

Play Long-Term Games

Life is the ultimate long game.

Those with low time preference play it more effectively—they happily delay gratification to allow compounding to work its magic.

In a world of people seeking instant gratification, this is a meaningful edge.

Speak Up

Closed mouths don’t get fed.

A little push goes a long way. Don't sit back and wait for good things to happen.

If you want something—and you’ve put in the work for it—ask for it.

Worst case: you’re told no and nothing has changed.

Best case: it’s yours.

Adopt a Positive Sum Mentality

Want to get ahead in life? Start genuinely rooting for others to succeed.

When one of us wins, we all win—winning spreads.

If you adopt that mentality, you’ll become a magnet for the highest quality people.

Operate in Your Zone of Genius

Your Zone of Genius is where your interests, passions and skills align.

Operating in your Zone of Genius means playing games you are uniquely well-suited to win.

Once you identify it, you can stop playing *their* games and start playing *yours*.

22/ Stop fearing rejection: You will not reach places if you are scared of getting a 'no' as an answer. Be bold, open to failure, and hungry for success. Being proactive is everything. Raise your hand. What's the worst that can happen?

21/ Work-life balance > Hustle: I believed the opposite but later realized that one’s hustle will be short-lived without a stable life. Burnouts are real. Work is not life. Ambition is great, but happiness from little things is greater. If you disagree, give it a few more years.

20/ Leadership is not office politics: Don't be someone who thrives on groupism, idea snatching, and blame games. You will be identified sooner or later. Rather, pull up people who are skilled and have high intent. Output > everything.

19/ Try being a generalist and specialist intermittently: Being a jack of all trades and master at none is not a bad thing. Mastery is tough. Always try to have a T-shaped career. Become the best at your craft and widen your skills over time.

18/ Be a good storyteller: You may be great at what you do, but if you can’t narrate a story about it, then people will forget. Even failures need narratives. I spoke at our annual conf in Ottawa & earned a standing ovation from thousands. It helped me make a global network.

17/ Be grateful: Be thankful to the people who have helped you in your journey. Even if they do not support you on multiple occasions, learn how they think first. Probe their decision but avoid questioning their judgment.

16/ Know if you are a pioneer, settler, or town planner: Some are good at the 0-to-1, some capitalize on that foundation, and few know how to build an empire out of it. Double down on your core competence, learn the others over time. https://t.co/YiqA9U6YRs

Starting a business can be painful.

You feel lost 97% of the time - the ups and downs are gut-wrenching.

I wish I had a cheat sheet of principles for my first startup.

So I wrote one.

Distribution is a bigger moat than product for startups. A really great product always loses to better distribution.

Sunk Cost Fallacy

Sunk costs are the economic costs already invested in an activity that cannot be recovered.

The fallacy is found in thinking that you should continue on the basis of all that you've put in, with no regard for future costs or likelihood of ultimate success.

Life has been hard. The last 18 months have only held horror after horror on average. 2020 was tough, 2021 has arguably been tougher. But serendipity can change things quite drastically. There is very little permanence in this world. Pick up that phone call. (n/n)

And I have changed. My understanding of engineering and how it makes the world revolve has improved. I have made many new friends, and people in the startup world have been amazing and supportive, always helping and guiding someone new in the system. (7/n)

It's June. It's been 180 days (or less). I have now been sitting in a different room for the past 3 months. But things have changed, and how! Co-founding @ShovelCompany has been one of the most memorable journeys, and we're only getting started. (6/n)

But it was as pure and real as it was magical. The unclarity of it all was overwhelming (ala Alice?) but here I was, fitting in and enjoying it. When you ask the most successful founders, they say starting up is a way of life. I didn't really understand this until I did. (5/n)

I didn't understand this world too well, here I was, an MBA grad, with sales and commercial transformation experience in large organisations, trying to fathom how a business entity can function without the financial clout or baggage of a large beast behind it. (4/n)

It started off simply as a way to relax, find something new and exciting. I was spending my afternoons in a room full of the smartest folks - discussing how we could make the world better. I was shaken, it was a far cry from sitting in front of a laptop screen all day. (3/n)

A call from @Sinister_Light had me packing my bags and traveling to Bangalore for a change of scene. I would collect my thoughts, organize my feelings, get clarity on what next and move on. It was a simple 4 week vacation. What could change? (2/n)

Just last December, I was in Mumbai, having spent 9 months in the same room. Mental and physical energy were at an ATL, and I wasn't really sure what I wanted to do next - listlessness and lethargy had overcome all my positive bias for action. (1/n)

7. Keep a flat hierarchy

Giving out vanity titles "CxO, VP of X, etc" early on makes you come off less reputable & can also be detrimental when hiring senior leadership.

If everyone in the org is considered equal, your teams' willingness to lean in & share––feels more welcomed.

8. Limit your distractions as a Founder

Avoid conferences. Avoid podcasts. Limit your use on social media.

Your time is better spent at your desk and/or with your team.

Anyone who’s been self employed for more than a year basically sees work reality differently. 

If you’ve done it for more than a year you know you can do it again even in a job. And that things will sort of work out

The person you marry is the person you fight with.

The house you buy is the house you repair.

The dream job you take is the job you stress over.

Everything comes with an inherent sacrifice—whatever makes us feel good will also inevitably make us feel bad.

Meaningful work has been co-opted by corporate HR departments to recruit people but then doing a sit and switch of a bundle of tasks good and bad. 

This is always what work is. Things we’d rather not do and stuff we enjoy. Paid and unpaid. 

The narratives  nudge us to blot out…

People might think im a fan of finding “meaningful work”

I mostly think aiming at something like this is a trap

I think much more useful is having a coherent narrative of how work fits in your life and then just a brutally honest account of how it feels when you do said work

I turned 40 last month, been running a startup for 5 years, and married for 15. 

Thought this is a good time to spew some gyan on turning old(er), being a founder, and giving one piece of advice on marriage. 
(1/n)
And here's the blog post https://t.co/1ndcAtARMK

Getting customers to use your product is a different ball game altogether. Discounting seems to be the preferred way today. 

But as a bootstrapped company you need to provide value and get customers to pay for it. It’s hard, but fun. This is what keeps me going.
(13/n)

Getting customers is the easier part. Using marketing to get your customers is a function of how much cash you have. 

The hard part is figuring out how to get the right customers and at the lowest cost. This is a make or break activity.
(12/n)

As a founder, if you’re constantly doing stuff, then stop. You need time to think. 

Once in a while, it’s important to get up and not know what you’re going to do today. That’s how you improve the business. If you’re just turning the clacks, something’s wrong.
(9/n)

To build a company, you have to do things that don’t make sense. If it made sense, everyone would be doing it. 

As your company grows most people in your company also want to do what others are doing. Social comfort is hugely underestimated in decision making.
(8/n)

If you’ve built tech at a startup and have not been involved in building the business, then you have not really been exposed to the real difficulties of starting up. Tech is what scales the business but if there’s no business, what are you scaling.
(7/n)

If you’ve been in tech all your life, my friend, you don’t understand business. My respect for folks who’ve built their company from scratch, even small brick and mortar shops, has grown manifold. A lot of techies think only they do real work, everyone else does “timepass”.
(6/n)

Unless you’re earning money for time rendered, bootstrapping is hard. Bootstrapping also requires capital. Duh! This was idiotic, but ya, I didn’t plan for this. 

Even if you’re not looking for investors, look for money. Having enough cash, is the #1 priority for a CEO.
(5/n)

I had no clue what I got myself into. The saddest part is that it took me a long time to realise I didn’t know jackshit.

Be always aware that you don’t know much. You learn by observing, listening to advice or going through stuff. I did the last.
(3/n)

7/ Every startup is it's own unique puzzle. The founders job is to decipher and figure out their puzzle. And like when you're assembling a puzzle, you sometimes get it wrong, and have to go back and try again, or try a different approach.

4/ I've seen this in many companies, but the experience with building @HiHello has put this progressive discovery of product at the front of my mind. The ideas start somewhere, they twist and morph, and then they eventually connect. And when they connect, it's magical!

3/ The metaphor I use to explain this to founders is "Sometimes there is no direct flight and you have to take a connecting flight." You can't get there from here, unless you go in some other direction first.

1/ So much of building a product/company is about progressive discovery. You build something, you figure out something else, then you build that, and so on. But you only figure out what to build by taking the first step, which may not always be *exactly* in the right direction.

Every day, I'll share a piece of my journey as I find my way back to my childhood passion.
I have to mention @AliAbdaal at this point, whose journey from the Medical field to becoming a successful YouTuber while following his passions has been a massive inspiration.

So, with pencils in hand, I've decided to challenge myself:
100 days of drawing cartoons and anime characters and anything else that brought immense joy to my younger, carefree self.
#100DaysOfSketching
I began reflecting on my childhood passions, and rediscovered my love for sketching 🎨 - something I had put on hold for over 15 years while I made my “career”.
Moving to such an environment granted me space and time to think about what I truly love doing, what makes me happy without burning me out or causing undue stress.

Post travel, we wanted to live in an environment closer to nature and away from the rush,noise and pollution of the cities. We moved to live in the mountains🏔️, where watching the sunrise/sunsets and sleeping at 9pm is the norm,proving that life could be more than just the grind.

So, I hit pause on my medical career and spent a year travelling through Europe & SEA 🌏 with my partner. Discovering cultures, meeting people, trying every delicious food I could find. For the first time in a long time, I felt truly alive and slept worry-free without stress.

Working under extreme conditions,looking at my peers do the same made me realize I was on a "default path"- more degrees, more work, less me. It didn’t feel right, yet I had no concrete answers on “what else?”

Around this time, I came across @p_millerd 's book The Pathless Path.

No Morning Meetings

Still blows my mind that reasoning ability comes out of next word prediction

then, get your first reference customer.

this means a customer that pays full list price, is using the product as you expect, and would happily recommend you to others.

if you manage this five times – congratulations, you've completed the product-market fit game.

the goal here is to see if people actually care, as quickly as possible.

there's no substitute for seeing what people do instead of what they say.

the idea, and the types of users you're targeting, will greatly influence your life.

i recommend trying out a bunch of ideas and then figuring out what you like and why.

*** level 1: find a significant problem to work on ***

start by solving a problem you've both had, and actually tried to solve.

otherwise, it can't be a significant problem.

nb:

keep detailed notes on all the conversations you have with customers at each level.

you may want to come back to a detail, and your notes may be useful for a later.

you might find you cannot complete a level. this is a sign you need to pivot your entire company.

Tip 2: Do not talk about your product or problems you have identified.

We simply asked them if they were facing any challenges because of the shift to remote. When they mentioned people related challenges, we gradually zoomed in🔬

E.g. If you ask a potential user "Hey, I'm thinking of creating this product X. Will you use it?"

Weirdly most folks that I talk to 1-1 are put off by the idea of 'merely' 10X-ing savings in 5 years, which is super aggressive from my pov. What surfaces is a desire for 100-100X in 2 years, which is a total fantasy.

The definition of sustainable is up to you, but for me it definitely includes the following expectations and constraints:
- 5+ year cycle time
- minimize vulnerability to obvious negative black swan sources like laws, regulations, politics etc
- should ratchet my savings

Instead of searching for startup ideas, search for ways to sustainably 10X your current savings.

In mid-2021, I came across the concept of "sang wenhua", a growing Chinese youth sub-culture centred on pessimism and apathy.

This evolved into a counter culture movement that involved lying down and doing as little as possible - tangping.

Frustrated by the mounting uncertainties and lack of economic opportunities, the Chinese youth are resorting to a new buzzword – bai lan (摆烂, or let it rot in English) – to capture their attitude towards life.

Just take a look at the companies reported to be integrating with the ONDC

Amazon
Flipkart
Dunzo
Paytm
All big banks (SBI, PNB, Kotak etc)
Reliance Retail
Google
PhonePe

However, something strange is happening. 

The companies who are the most eager to integrate with ONDC are the ones who will *seemingly* lose power and influence once an open network comes into place.

First, a bit about the ONDC. It's been called many things. 

Like Amazon slayer. 

Or a way to democratise e-commerce in India. 

Or the UPI moment for e-commerce. 

The core premise is —take what’s closed and proprietary, and make it open and standard.

India is quietly building an Amazon and Flipkart killer. It’s called the ONDC, and it’s purpose is to shift power away from all the incumbents.

At least that’s the plan.

In reality, the ones who are the most interested in joining it, are…the incumbents. 

But why?

When I joined the call, they were making fun of me and how small the Adaface team was (they stopped when they realized I joined). 

1 week ago they purchased one of our largest subscription plans.

You’re not just building a startup. The startup is also building you.

Here are 7️⃣ pieces of advice you won’t hear often but will realize as you grow.

Be nice to people. Building a startup is hard, so you’ll need people to help you on the way up, which will be the same ones that’ll keep you stable when you’re there. They make films about crazy CEOs like Neumann and Holmes, but the happiest and kindest ones they’ll never dare.

Met a person who explained how he has been hiring people by using company’s taxi drivers. If he wants to hire oracle experts, he pays oracle taxi drivers to pitch for him and share his number. He pays them Rs.500 in advance and then Rs.150 for every relevant call. What a hack 🤯

@aymanalabdul Have A North Star

And actually follow it. Each company needs a big hairy goal they obsess on day in and day out. 

I got my team one of these that counts emails. 
North star, grow this, and it auto populates to our list. https://t.co/kJx8opYgl0

@aymanalabdul The best leaders don't fire, they persuade.

He told me, if you’re wondering if you should fire them, you should. 

BUT, the best leaders fire less & coach people to move on and find what's right for them more.

Not the right hire for you is right for another.

@aymanalabdul Owners Own Execution

I had a "trouble" child I kept having to micromanage. I wanted to pull my hair out. 

He said, "It's not his fault; it's yours. What you allow to continue will continue."

Poor Execution is only one person's fault... yours. 

Own it, you'll stop it.

There is difference between a great manager and the manager you like.

Some traits of great managers
1. Great manager will not lay down the growth path for you, instead will provide you with the right opportunities to grow on the path you decide to grow.
2. If possible mentor you personally. If not possible due to time or expert constraints will point to the right person in the org to learn from and provide you with opportunities to work with them.
3. Will give to freedom and power to take decisions with sense of accountability.
4. Be candor with you. Will praise you in public and give you feedback in private.

The #1 lesson I’ve learned in entrepreneurship: 

Success isn’t a result of working harder or smarter. It’s a result of working on the right things.

[2] Say No to No-Agenda Meetings

Serendipity seldom hits.

Learn to say ‘No’ to meetings that don’t have a strong agenda.

It saves you both time and energy to do the tasks that require your attention more.
